#### You said:

sop creation for Easter cape wait for proper scope

  

#### ChatGPT said:

Understood. I’ll hold off on generating the full SOP until you define the scope.

To streamline development once you’re ready, here is the **minimum scope definition** I’ll need (keep it concise but specific):

---

### 1\. Operation Type

- Aquaponics system type (e.g., media bed, DWC, NFT, hybrid)
- Scale (pilot / commercial / research)
- Location specifics within Eastern Cape (climate affects SOPs)

---

### 2\. System Components in Scope

Specify which modules need SOPs:

- Fish production (species, tanks)
- Plant production (crop types)
- Water management & filtration
- Electrical & automation
- Feeding systems
- Biosecurity
- Harvesting & processing
- Maintenance routines

---

### 3\. Target Users

- Skilled technicians / general labor / management
- Required level of technical detail (basic vs engineering-grade SOP)

---

### 4\. Compliance Requirements

- Any standards (e.g., HACCP, GlobalG.A.P., local regulations)

---

### 5\. Output Format Preference

- Step-by-step operational SOP
- Checklists
- Flow diagrams
- Maintenance schedules
- Emergency procedures

---

### 6\. Key Constraints

- Budget limitations
- Power reliability (important in Eastern Cape)
- Water source (municipal, borehole, rain-fed)

---

### 7\. Priority Areas (if phasing)

Indicate what to build first:

- Example: “Start with fish system SOP only” or “Full farm operations”

---

Once you provide this, I will generate:

- Structured SOP documents (modular)
- Daily / weekly / monthly operational schedules
- Failure-mode procedures (critical for aquaponics stability)
- Optional: automation logic (if you’re integrating control systems)

Provide the scope when ready.

#### ChatGPT said:

This is sufficient to begin a **Version 1 SOP framework**. I will structure it so you can append operational refinements over time and insert your dictated rotation logic later.

Below is a **modular SOP (draft v1)** aligned to your infrastructure and production goal.

---

# **STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE (SOP)**

## Tilapia Production System – Eastern Cape Aquaponics

### **Document Type**

Living SOP (incrementally updated)

### **Primary Objective**

Produce consistent monthly yield (~1000 kg Tilapia) through controlled hatchery → nursery → grow-out progression with defined grading and rotation.

---

# **1\. SYSTEM OVERVIEW**

### **Infrastructure Summary**

- **Hatchery**
	- 2 × 8L overflow incubation cups
		- 2 × 20L fry holding tanks
		- Integrated:
		- Temperature control
				- Matala filtration
				- Aeration system
- **Nursery**
	- 8 × 2,500L tanks
		- Dual-batch capacity per tank (if required)
- **Grow-out**
	- 4 × 30,000L tanks
- **Species**
	- Tilapia (assumed Nile unless specified)

---

# **2\. PRODUCTION STRATEGY (BASELINE)**

### **Target**

- 1000 kg fish/month

### **Biological Assumptions (to refine later)**

- Survival rate: 50–70% (to be validated)
- Harvest weight: 500–800 g
- Estimated fish required: ~1,500–2,000 fish/month

### **Egg Production Strategy**

- Target: ~2,000 eggs/month (adjust after survival validation)
- Continuous staggered production (NOT batch-only)

---

# **3\. PROCESS FLOW**

### **Stage 1: Hatchery**

Egg → fry (0–2 weeks)

### **Stage 2: Nursery**

Fry → fingerlings → juveniles (2–10 weeks)

### **Stage 3: Grow-out**

Juveniles → harvest size

---

# **4\. HATCHERY SOP**

## **4.1 Daily Tasks**

- Check water temperature (target: 27–30°C)
- Verify aeration in cups and holding tanks
- Inspect egg tumbling (no clumping)
- Remove dead eggs/fry
- Check flow-through system (overflow working)
- Observe fry behavior (uniform swimming)

## **4.2 Weekly Tasks**

- Clean Matala filters
- Inspect all air lines and stones
- Calibrate temperature control system
- Record hatch rate (%)

## **4.3 Monthly Tasks**

- Evaluate hatch success vs target
- Adjust egg production volume
- Deep clean system (non-disruptive rotation)

---

# **5\. NURSERY SOP**

## **5.1 Stocking Rules**

- Max density to be defined (recommend starting conservative)
- Up to **2 batches per tank** (only if sizes are compatible)

## **5.2 Grading Strategy**

- Monthly grading mandatory
- Separate by size:
	- Fast growers
		- Medium
		- Slow

## **5.3 Daily Tasks**

- Check dissolved oxygen (critical)
- Observe feeding response
- Remove mortalities
- Inspect fish size uniformity
- Check inflow/outflow
- Verify aeration

## **5.4 Weekly Tasks**

- Partial tank inspection (biofilm, sludge)
- Check stocking density vs growth
- Adjust feeding rates
- Inspect structural integrity (tanks, valves)

## **5.5 Monthly Tasks**

- **Full grading operation**
- Reallocate fish to next tanks
- Update biomass per tank
- Cull weak or deformed fish
- Prepare next batch intake

---

# **6\. GROW-OUT SOP**

## **6.1 Stocking**

- Only graded, uniform juveniles enter grow-out

## **6.2 Daily Tasks**

- Monitor DO, temperature, and fish behavior
- Check feeding efficiency (no excess waste)
- Inspect for disease signs
- Confirm aeration redundancy

## **6.3 Weekly Tasks**

- Biomass estimation
- Adjust feed conversion strategy
- Inspect tank sludge levels
- Check structural and pipe integrity

## **6.4 Monthly Tasks**

- Partial harvest planning
- Growth rate evaluation
- Tank rotation preparation
- Market alignment (harvest timing)

---

# **7\. TANK ROTATION FRAMEWORK (PLACEHOLDER)**

(To be completed based on your dictated logic)

Will include:

- Batch movement schedule
- Tank allocation per month
- Overlap strategy (continuous production)
- Density thresholds per stage

---

# **8\. MORTALITY & LOSS MANAGEMENT**

## **Tracking**

- Daily mortality log per tank
- Hatch rate tracking
- Survival % per stage

## **Adjustment Rule**

If survival drops below threshold:

- Increase egg production
- Reduce stocking density
- Investigate water quality immediately

---

# **9\. FEED MANAGEMENT (BASELINE)**

(To refine later)

- Hatchery: minimal / yolk stage
- Nursery: high protein starter feed
- Grow-out: FCR optimization

Track:

- Feed input per tank
- Growth vs feed ratio

---

# **10\. RECORD KEEPING (CRITICAL)**

Each tank must track:

- Stocking date
- Number of fish
- Size class
- Mortality
- Feed input
- Movement history

---

# **11\. KEY RISKS**

- Oxygen crashes (highest risk)
- Overcrowding due to dual batching
- Uneven growth (requires strict grading)
- Hatchery inefficiency impacting entire pipeline

---

# **WHAT I STILL NEED FROM YOU (NEXT STEP)**

To complete a **precise tank rotation + production model**, I need:

### 1\. Target harvest weight

(e.g., 500g, 700g, 1kg)

### 2\. Expected grow-out duration

(e.g., 4 months, 6 months)

### 3\. Preferred stocking density (if you have one)

### 4\. Feeding strategy (manual / automated)

### 5\. Your intended **tank rotation logic**

You mentioned:

> “I would like the SOP to include the tank rotation plan as I dictate it”

Provide that next.

---

Once you give that, I will:

- Build a **month-by-month rotation schedule**
- Map exact fish flow across all tanks
- Align egg production → harvest output mathematically
- Refine this SOP into an operational production system

Proceed with your rotation plan.

#### ChatGPT said:

You’ve done a substantial amount of the production modeling already. The structure is usable, but there are a few **critical corrections and optimizations** required before locking this into an SOP—mainly around **stage duration, density realism, and system utilization**.

I will do three things:

1. **Correct your biological + system assumptions**
2. **Translate your table into a workable tank rotation model**
3. **Embed this into the SOP (updated sections)**

---

# **1\. CRITICAL CORRECTIONS (NON-NEGOTIABLE)**

## **1.1 Hatchery Duration – Incorrect**

> You proposed ~2 months in hatchery

**Correction:**

- Hatchery phase = **3–10 days only**
- Fry holding = **max 2–3 weeks total**

**Reason:**

- Keeping fish too long in hatchery causes:
	- Stunting
		- Oxygen instability
		- Poor survival downstream

**Action:**

- Move fish to nursery **by week 2–3 max**

---

## **1.2 Stocking Density – 60 kg/m³**

This is **aggressive but possible** *only if:*

- Strong aeration redundancy
- Good solids management
- Strict grading discipline

**Recommendation:**

- Nursery: **20–40 kg/m³**
- Grow-out: **40–60 kg/m³ (only at final stage)**

---

## **1.3 Production Reality Check**

You want:

- **1000 kg/month harvest**
- **10–11 month cycle**

This means:

- You must run a **staggered 10–11 batch pipeline**
- Not single batch

---

# **2\. REFINED PRODUCTION MODEL**

## **Core Principle**

Each month:

- New batch enters system
- One batch exits system

---

# **3\. TANK UTILIZATION MODEL (CORRECTED)**

## **HATCHERY (Weeks 0–3)**

- 2 × 8L → egg incubation
- 2 × 20L → fry holding

**Flow:**

- Continuous egg production (weekly)
- NOT monthly batch

---

## **NURSERY (Month 1–4)**

You have:

- 8 × 2500L tanks

### **Correct Use**

Each tank becomes a **stage buffer**, not just a container

| Stage | Age | Tanks |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Early fry | Month 1 | 2 tanks |
| Fingerling | Month 2 | 2 tanks |
| Advanced fingerling | Month 3 | 2 tanks |
| Pre-growout | Month 4 | 2 tanks |

✔ This uses all 8 tanks efficiently  
✔ Allows grading separation

---

## **GROW-OUT (Month 5–11)**

You have:

- 4 × 30,000L tanks

### **Problem**

You need **~7 months grow-out**, but only 4 tanks

### **Solution (Required)**

Each tank must hold:

- **2 staggered cohorts**

| Tank | Cohort A | Cohort B |
| --- | --- | --- |
| GT1 | Month 5–7 | Month 8–11 |
| GT2 | Month 6–8 | Month 9–11 |
| GT3 | Month 7–9 | Month 10–11 |
| GT4 | Month 8–10 | Month 11 harvest |

✔ This is the only way to hit monthly harvest targets

---

# **4\. TANK ROTATION LOGIC (SOP INSERT)**

## **4.1 Monthly Movement Rule**

Every month:

1. Hatchery → Nursery Tank Group 1
2. Nursery shift forward (T1 → T2 → T3 → T4)
3. Month 4 fish → Grow-out tanks
4. Oldest grow-out batch → Harvest

---

## **4.2 Grading Rule (CRITICAL)**

At each monthly move:

- Split into:
	- Fast growers → advance
		- Slow growers → stay back

✔ Prevents biomass bottlenecks  
✔ Improves feed efficiency

---

## **4.3 Density Control Rule**

Never exceed:

- Nursery: **40 kg/m³**
- Grow-out early: **30–40 kg/m³**
- Grow-out final: **≤60 kg/m³**

---

# **5\. DAILY / WEEKLY / MONTHLY CHECKLISTS (REFINED)**

## **5.1 DAILY CHECKLIST**

### **All Systems**

- Temperature (record)
- Dissolved Oxygen (visual + meter if available)
- Fish behavior (feeding response is primary indicator)
- Mortalities removed + logged
- Aeration check (redundancy functioning)
- Water flow (no blockages)

### **Feeding**

- Feed 2–3 times/day (manual)
- Observe:
	- Uneaten feed
		- Aggression
		- Weak feeders

---

## **5.2 WEEKLY CHECKLIST**

- Clean filters (Matala + system)
- Inspect all airlines + diffusers
- Biomass estimation per tank
- Adjust feed rates
- Inspect sludge accumulation
- Check tank structural integrity

---

## **5.3 MONTHLY CHECKLIST (CRITICAL OPERATIONS)**

### **1\. Grading**

- Separate fish by size
- Redistribute across tanks

### **2\. Tank Rotation**

- Move all batches forward one stage

### **3\. Biomass Audit**

- Kg per tank
- Density verification

### **4\. Mortality Review**

- Compare expected vs actual survival

### **5\. Production Alignment**

- Confirm:
	- Next harvest volume
		- Hatchery production matches demand

---

# **6\. EGG PRODUCTION STRATEGY (CORRECTED)**

## **Your assumption:**

> 2000 eggs → 1000 kg

This is **too optimistic**

### **Recommended Planning:**

| Stage | Survival |
| --- | --- |
| Egg → Fry | 70–80% |
| Fry → Fingerling | 60–70% |
| Fingerling → Harvest | 70–80% |

### **Realistic Output:**

- 2000 eggs → ~600–900 harvest fish

### **Recommendation:**

👉 Produce **3000–3500 eggs/month**

---

# **7\. FEED STRATEGY (MANUAL)**

- Early stage: 5–6 feeds/day (small amounts)
- Nursery: 3–4 feeds/day
- Grow-out: 2–3 feeds/day

Track:

- Feed vs growth
- Adjust based on appetite, not fixed table only

---

# **8\. KEY SYSTEM RISKS (UPDATED)**

1. **Overcrowding from dual batching**
2. **Under-grading → growth suppression**
3. **Oxygen crash at high density**
4. **Hatchery bottleneck limiting entire system**
5. **Feed inefficiency due to mixed sizes**

---

# **NEXT STEP (IMPORTANT)**

Before I finalize this into a **clean, client-ready SOP document**, I need you to confirm or adjust:

### 1\. Do you accept:

- Hatchery reduced to ~2–3 weeks?
- Nursery = 4 months total?

### 2\. Grow-out:

- Are you okay running **2 cohorts per grow-out tank**?

### 3\. Egg production:

- Increase to ~3000/month?

---

Once confirmed, I will:

- Convert this into a **formal, client-facing SOP**
- Add:
	- Clean formatting
		- Tables for tank rotation (month-by-month)
		- Printable checklists
		- Expansion sections for future updates

Then we’ll refine further with your real-world data.

  

#### You said:

in the planthat you have given me i dont see the nursory having mu yegg production included why is that and i agree lets puch to hit the 3500 egg target a month

  

#### ChatGPT said:

You are correct. I left **broodstock egg production** out of the nursery flow, and that was a design gap.

The reason is that I treated the nursery only as a **juvenile rearing section**. But in your system, the nursery is doing **two functions**:

1. **Broodstock / breeding and egg collection**
2. **Fry, fingerling, and juvenile nursery rearing**

So the SOP must explicitly define the nursery as a **combined breeding and rearing zone**. Since you want to produce your own eggs, that section cannot be separate or assumed.

We will now lock in **3,500 eggs per month** as the planning target.

---

# Revised SOP Structure Update

## Nursery Section Must Be Split Into 2 Subsections

### A. Broodstock and Egg Production

### B. Fry/Fingerling/Juvenile Rearing

That is the correct structure for your client SOP.

---

# 1\. NURSERY AS A BROODSTOCK AND EGG PRODUCTION AREA

## Purpose

Maintain selected male and female tilapia broodstock for ongoing spawning and monthly egg production to support downstream hatchery, nursery, and grow-out targets.

## Monthly Production Target

- **Target egg production:** 3,500 eggs/month
- Purpose: support losses across hatchery, nursery, grading, and grow-out so harvest targets remain achievable

---

# 2\. BROODSTOCK FUNCTION IN THE NURSERY

## Operational Role

The nursery must contain a defined broodstock section where:

- breeders are maintained,
- spawning activity is monitored,
- females are checked for eggs,
- eggs are collected and transferred to hatchery incubation cups,
- broodfish are rotated and conditioned.

## Why this matters

Without a formal broodstock section:

- egg production becomes inconsistent,
- hatchery loading becomes irregular,
- monthly harvest planning breaks down.

---

# 3\. RECOMMENDED NURSERY ZONING

You have **8 nursery tanks at 2,500 L each**.

A workable Version 1 allocation is:

- **2 tanks** = broodstock / breeding
- **6 tanks** = nursery rearing and grading

This is the cleanest starting point.

## Proposed Layout

### Tank N1-N2

- Broodstock breeding tanks
- Mouthbrooding female management
- Egg collection and breeder conditioning

### Tank N3-N4

- Early fry / post-hatch transition

### Tank N5-N6

- Fingerling stage

### Tank N7-N8

- Juvenile / pre-grow-out stage

This can be adjusted later, but it gives the SOP a real operating logic.

---

# 4\. BROODSTOCK SOP SECTION

## 4.1 Broodstock Selection

Use only healthy, active fish with:

- good body shape,
- no deformities,
- strong feeding response,
- no visible disease,
- acceptable growth history.

Avoid keeping weak, slow, or damaged fish as breeders.

---

## 4.2 Breeding Ratio

For tilapia, a practical starting point is:

- **1 male : 3 to 5 females**

A good initial SOP ratio:

- **1:4**

This can be optimized after observing fertility and aggression.

---

## 4.3 Egg Collection

Since tilapia are mouthbrooders:

- inspect brood females routinely,
- identify holding females,
- strip or collect eggs carefully according to handling protocol,
- transfer eggs immediately to hatchery cups.

## Collection Frequency

- Check breeding tanks **at least 2 to 3 times per week**
- During stable production, weekly egg count must be logged

To hit **3,500 eggs/month**, egg collection cannot be casual. It must be a scheduled production activity.

---

# 5\. BROODSTOCK DAILY / WEEKLY / MONTHLY TASKS

## Daily Checklist – Broodstock Tanks

- Check water temperature
- Check aeration and flow
- Observe spawning behavior
- Identify females suspected of holding eggs
- Check broodstock feeding response
- Remove mortalities if any
- Record abnormal behavior, aggression, or injury

## Weekly Checklist – Broodstock Tanks

- Inspect body condition of broodstock
- Separate injured or overly aggressive fish
- Check breeding ratio remains correct
- Count holding females
- Record eggs collected for the week
- Review broodstock feed input

## Monthly Checklist – Broodstock Tanks

- Calculate total eggs produced
- Compare against target of **3,500 eggs/month**
- Remove underperforming breeders if necessary
- Replace with selected candidates if needed
- Review fertility, hatch rate, and survival trend
- Adjust broodstock numbers if target is not being met

---

# 6\. UPDATED SYSTEM FLOW

The correct production flow is now:

**Broodstock in nursery → eggs collected → hatchery incubation → fry holding → nursery rearing/grading → grow-out → harvest**

That closes the loop properly.

---

# 7\. IMPORTANT CORRECTION TO THE TANK PLAN

My previous version assumed all 8 nursery tanks were available for juvenile rearing stages.  
That was incomplete because it ignored the broodstock requirement.

Now that broodstock is included, the nursery rearing side has **6 tanks available**, unless:

- broodstock share space with some rearing phase temporarily, or
- you later dedicate another area for breeders.

So the production model must now account for:

- **2 nursery tanks reserved for egg production**
- **6 nursery tanks for batch progression**

That is still workable, but it tightens nursery

  

#### You said:

ok be fore we continue finaising please can you tell me if you think 1000kgs is realistic or should i down scale to 600kgs rather or 800kgs what is the most safe realistic number you would suggest

  

#### ChatGPT said:

My recommendation is **plan commercially around 800 kg/month**, not 1,000 kg/month, for the first operating phase.

That is the safest realistic number given your current layout, manual feeding, in-house egg production target of 3,500 eggs/month, long 10–11 month cycle, and the fact that the nursery is doing both broodstock and rearing functions. Tilapia systems can be run at high densities, but once density rises, water quality stability and disease risk become less forgiving; 60 kg/m³ is feasible, but it is not a conservative planning basis for a new production program. In RAS, around 60 kg/m³ is broadly within intensive practice, while higher densities demand tighter control and become less stable operationally. [Florida Shellfish Guide+1](https://shellfish.ifas.ufl.edu/wp-content/uploads/Handout-3_SRAC_0453_Review-of-Current-Design-Practices_Recirculating-Aquaculture-Systems.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

Here is the practical way to think about it:

At **800 kg/month**, assuming a harvest weight of **600–800 g/fish**, you need to harvest roughly **1,000 to 1,333 fish per month**. With **3,500 eggs/month**, that means an overall egg-to-harvest survival of about **29% to 38%**. That is demanding, but still within a realistic operating envelope for a system that grades monthly and manages losses properly. By contrast, **1,000 kg/month** would require roughly **1,250 to 1,667 fish per month**, which pushes required overall survival to about **36% to 48%** from egg to harvest. That is possible, but it is no longer the “safe” number; it becomes a performance target. [FAOHome+1](https://www.fao.org/fishery/culturedspecies/oreochromis_niloticus?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

I would frame the production targets like this:

- **600 kg/month** = conservative and very achievable startup target
- **800 kg/month** = safe realistic commercial target
- **1,000 kg/month** = stretch target once the SOP is proven in real operation

The main reason I would not anchor the SOP at 1,000 kg/month yet is not only tank volume. It is the combination of **broodstock productivity, hatch consistency, grading discipline, and manual feeding**. Nile tilapia have relatively low fecundity compared with many farmed fish; egg output depends heavily on female size, and females stop feeding while incubating. FAO also notes that monosex grow-out allows higher stocking rates and better uniformity, which matters because mixed-sex production usually depresses growth performance and consistency. [FAOHome+2FAOHome+2](https://www.fao.org/4/t8389e/T8389E10.htm?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

From a system-capacity perspective, your **grow-out volume** is not the main bottleneck. Four 30,000 L tanks give you **120 m³** of grow-out water, so even at 60 kg/m³ final density the theoretical standing biomass ceiling is around **7,200 kg**. The constraint is whether the hatchery-plus-broodstock-plus-nursery pipeline can reliably feed that grow-out capacity every month without size variation, stunting, oxygen pressure, and handling losses eroding performance. [Florida Shellfish Guide+1](https://shellfish.ifas.ufl.edu/wp-content/uploads/Handout-3_SRAC_0453_Review-of-Current-Design-Practices_Recirculating-Aquaculture-Systems.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

My exact recommendation:

**Set the client SOP target at 800 kg/month.**  
Then define:

- **Operational base target:** 800 kg/month
- **Minimum acceptable target:** 600 kg/month
- **Expansion target after data validation:** 1,000 kg/month

That gives you a commercially credible SOP without overpromising.

A useful wording for the SOP would be:

> “The production system is designed to operate at a base target of 800 kg harvested Tilapia per month, with 600 kg/month regarded as the minimum stable output during optimization and 1,000 kg/month treated as an advanced target subject to demonstrated broodstock performance, hatch success, survival, grading efficiency, and water-quality stability.”

One more point: if you are not producing **all-male or predominantly male grow-out fish**, then 800 kg becomes even more sensible, because mixed-sex tilapia tend to give less uniform growth and lower grow-out efficiency over long cycles. [FAOHome](https://www.fao.org/fishery/docs/CDrom/T583/root/06.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

My advice is to finalize the SOP around **800 kg/month**, then build the tables so that scaling to 1,000 kg later is a revision, not a rewrite.

  

#### You said:

ok after reading your documents i think the answer would be to rather start at 600kgs and thenscale up as my customer gains more experience . in the final sop make sure to give the water flow needed foe each tank

  

#### ChatGPT said:

Yes. I agree with that decision.

**Set the final SOP base target at 600 kg/month.**  
That is the most conservative and operationally realistic starting point for this system while the customer is still learning broodstock management, hatch consistency, grading discipline, and manual feeding. Once real survival, FCR, and growth data are proven over a few cycles, the SOP can be revised upward to 800 kg/month and later treated toward 1,000 kg/month as an expansion target. [Open Knowledge FAO+2FAOHome+2](https://openknowledge.fao.org/server/api/core/bitstreams/a0297773-095a-4ae7-9a89-5a3bfb48abc7/content?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

And yes: in the **final SOP I will include the required water flow for each tank**.

For now, I would base the flow section on two design rules:

1. **Hydraulic turnover rule:** tank systems commonly use enough flow to replace 100% of tank volume every **90–120 minutes** in flow-through style guidance. [Aquaculture](https://aquaculture.mgcafe.uky.edu/sites/aquaculture.ca.uky.edu/files/srac_282_tank_culture_of_tilapia.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
2. **Biomass support rule:** intensive tilapia culture has been reported at a minimum of about **0.50 L/min/kg biomass** for clean culture water, with another cited range of roughly **0.23–0.45 L/min/kg** from oxygen-support guidance. [FAOHome+1](https://www.fao.org/fishery/fr/openasfa/dc8455e9-acad-4546-ab10-af00815a7161?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

For the SOP, the practical engineering rule will be:

> **Required tank flow = whichever is greater of**
> 
> - **tank-volume turnover requirement**, or
> - **biomass-based flow requirement**.  
> 	Hatchery egg cups are the exception: those are set by **gentle egg suspension/tumbling**, not by a straight biomass formula. [Stellenbosch University+3Aquaculture+3FAOHome+3](https://aquaculture.mgcafe.uky.edu/sites/aquaculture.ca.uky.edu/files/srac_282_tank_culture_of_tilapia.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

## Provisional water-flow schedule to include in the SOP

### Hatchery

**8 L overflow egg cups**

- Set flow to achieve **continuous gentle tumbling/suspension of eggs**
- Provisional operating band: **about 0.1 to 1.0 L/min per cup**
- Final setpoint is visual: eggs must stay moving continuously, but not be blasted or pinned. Small incubators for tilapia are reported around **1 L/min**. [troutlodge.com+3FAOHome+3FAOHome+3](https://www.fao.org/4/t8389e/T8389E11.htm?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

**20 L fry holding tanks**

- At your stated max biomass of **1.2 kg**, the biomass rule gives **0.6 L/min minimum**. [FAOHome](https://www.fao.org/fishery/fr/openasfa/dc8455e9-acad-4546-ab10-af00815a7161?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
- I would specify an operating range of **0.6 to 2.0 L/min per tank**, increasing with biomass and feed load. This upper range is an engineering recommendation derived from the biomass rule and the need for extra flushing margin. [FAOHome+1](https://www.fao.org/fishery/fr/openasfa/dc8455e9-acad-4546-ab10-af00815a7161?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

### Nursery

**2,500 L tanks**

- Turnover basis: **20.8 to 27.8 L/min** for one full turnover every 120–90 minutes. [Aquaculture](https://aquaculture.mgcafe.uky.edu/sites/aquaculture.ca.uky.edu/files/srac_282_tank_culture_of_tilapia.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
- At your stated max biomass of **150 kg**, the biomass rule gives **75 L/min minimum**. [FAOHome](https://www.fao.org/fishery/fr/openasfa/dc8455e9-acad-4546-ab10-af00815a7161?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

So for the SOP I would write:

- **Broodstock nursery tanks:** **25 to 50 L/min per tank** as a practical starting band, then increase if broodstock biomass rises materially. This is an operational recommendation inferred from turnover guidance and the fact that broodstock tanks will usually run below the nursery’s maximum biomass. [Aquaculture+1](https://aquaculture.mgcafe.uky.edu/sites/aquaculture.ca.uky.edu/files/srac_282_tank_culture_of_tilapia.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
- **Fry/fingerling/juvenile nursery tanks:** **75 L/min per tank minimum at full loading**, with a working range of **40 to 75 L/min** depending on actual biomass in the tank. [FAOHome+1](https://www.fao.org/fishery/fr/openasfa/dc8455e9-acad-4546-ab10-af00815a7161?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

### Grow-out

**30,000 L tanks**

- Turnover basis: **250 to 333 L/min** for one full turnover every 120–90 minutes. [Aquaculture](https://aquaculture.mgcafe.uky.edu/sites/aquaculture.ca.uky.edu/files/srac_282_tank_culture_of_tilapia.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
- At your stated max biomass of **1,800 kg**, the biomass rule gives **900 L/min minimum**. [FAOHome](https://www.fao.org/fishery/fr/openasfa/dc8455e9-acad-4546-ab10-af00815a7161?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

So for the SOP I would specify:

- **Grow-out tanks:** **900 L/min per tank minimum at full biomass**, with lower staged flow allowed when the tank is lightly loaded. As an operating framework:
	- early grow-out: **250–450 L/min**
		- mid grow-out: **450–700 L/min**
		- late grow-out / near harvest: **700–900 L/min** [FAOHome+1](https://www.fao.org/fishery/fr/openasfa/dc8455e9-acad-4546-ab10-af00815a7161?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

## My recommendation on wording in the final SOP

I would not present flow as one fixed number only. I would present it as:

- **minimum required flow**
- **normal operating range**
- **increase trigger points** based on biomass, feeding rate, DO, and solids loading. That is technically more correct for a working tilapia SOP. [Open Knowledge FAO+1](https://openknowledge.fao.org/server/api/core/bitstreams/a0297773-095a-4ae7-9a89-5a3bfb48abc7/content?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

So the direction is now:

- **Base production target:** 600 kg/month
- **Scale-up target after experience:** 800 kg/month
- **Final SOP to include:** water flow per hatchery cup, fry tank, nursery tank, broodstock tank, and grow-out tank

The only caveat is this: because your hatchery is a compact integrated unit with its own Matala filtration and aeration, I will present the hatchery cup flow in the SOP as **performance-based adjustment** rather than pretending there is one universal exact liter-per-minute figure for all egg loads. That is the correct approach for tilapia egg incubation. [FAOHome+2FAOHome+2](https://www.fao.org/4/t8389e/T8389E11.htm?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

Send the word **continue** and I’ll assemble the full client-facing SOP structure around the **600 kg/month** production target with the flow section embedded.

#### ChatGPT said:

Understood.

We will set the SOP hydraulic standard to:

> **Minimum system flow: 1 complete water exchange per tank every 60 minutes**

That becomes the default design basis for all main production tanks unless a higher biomass-based flow requirement applies.

## Flow rates at 60-minute turnover

### Hatchery

- **8 L overflow cup:** **8 L/hour** = **0.13 L/min per cup**
- **20 L fry holding tank:** **20 L/hour** = **0.33 L/min per tank**

For the hatchery, I will still note in the SOP that the egg cups must be adjusted to maintain **gentle continuous egg movement**, because egg incubation is performance-based, not purely turnover-based.

### Nursery

- **2,500 L tank:** **2,500 L/hour** = **41.7 L/min per tank**

### Grow-out

- **30,000 L tank:** **30,000 L/hour** = **500 L/min per tank**

## Important technical note

A **60-minute turnover** is a strong and sensible standard for the nursery and grow-out side. It is more conservative than the 90–120 minute basis and gives better protection against oxygen stress, ammonia accumulation, and solids loading.

But I will structure the SOP so that the actual operating rule is:

> **Run each tank at a minimum of 1 water exchange per 60 minutes, and increase flow further when biomass, feeding rate, oxygen demand, or solids loading require it.**

That is the correct technical wording, because at higher biomass the tank may need more than the turnover minimum.

## What this means for your SOP

I will now lock these standards in:

- **Base production target:** 600 kg/month
- **Scale-up path:** later revision to 800 kg/month
- **Standard tank flow basis:** 1 water change every 60 minutes
- **Flow section included for:**
	- 8 L hatchery cups
		- 20 L fry holding tanks
		- 2,500 L nursery tanks
		- 30,000 L grow-out tanks

Also, because you want this as **one living SOP with expandable sections**, I will format the final document so future additions can slot into:

- Hatchery
- Nursery broodstock and egg production
- Nursery rearing
- Grow-out
- Grading and tank rotation
- Daily, weekly, and monthly checklists
- Water flow and water quality standards

Below is the **formal, client-ready SOP (Version 1)** structured as a **living document**. It incorporates:

---

# **STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE (SOP)**

## Tilapia Production System – Eastern Cape Aquaponics

### **Document Status**

Version 1 – Operational Baseline (Expandable)

---

# **1\. PRODUCTION OBJECTIVE**

## **Primary Target**

- **600 kg Tilapia harvested per month**

## **System Strategy**

- Continuous staggered production
- Monthly batch progression
- Integrated broodstock → hatchery → nursery → grow-out pipeline

## **Future Scaling**

- Phase 2: 800 kg/month
- Phase 3: 1000 kg/month (subject to performance validation)

---

# **2\. SYSTEM OVERVIEW**

## **Infrastructure**

### Hatchery

- 2 × 8L overflow egg incubation cups
- 2 × 20L fry holding tanks
- Integrated:
	- Temperature control
		- Matala filtration
		- Aeration

### Nursery

- 8 × 2,500L tanks
- Dual function:
	- Broodstock + egg production
		- Fry → fingerling → juvenile rearing

### Grow-out

- 4 × 30,000L tanks

---

# **3\. WATER FLOW STANDARD**

## **System Rule**

> Each tank must achieve **1 full water exchange every 60 minutes (minimum)**

## **Flow Rates**

| System | Tank Size | Flow Rate |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Hatchery cup | 8 L | 0.13 L/min |
| Fry tank | 20 L | 0.33 L/min |
| Nursery tank | 2,500 L | 41.7 L/min |
| Grow-out tank | 30,000 L | 500 L/min |

## **Operational Rule**

Increase flow if:

- Biomass increases
- Feeding increases
- Oxygen demand rises
- Solids accumulation is visible

## **Hatchery Exception**

Egg cups must maintain:

- Continuous gentle tumbling of eggs
- No settling or clumping

---

# **4\. PRODUCTION FLOW**

**Broodstock (Nursery) → Egg Collection → Hatchery → Fry → Nursery → Grow-out → Harvest**

---

# **5\. HATCHERY SOP**

## **Duration**

- Egg to fry: 3–10 days
- Fry holding: up to 2–3 weeks

## **Daily Tasks**

- Check temperature (27–30°C)
- Verify aeration and flow
- Ensure egg tumbling
- Remove dead eggs/fry
- Observe fry behavior

## **Weekly Tasks**

- Clean Matala filters
- Inspect air lines
- Check system stability
- Record hatch rate

## **Monthly Tasks**

- Evaluate hatch success
- Adjust egg production
- Deep clean system

---

# **6\. NURSERY SOP**

## **6.1 Nursery Zoning**

| Tanks | Function |
| --- | --- |
| N1–N2 | Broodstock / breeding |
| N3–N4 | Early fry |
| N5–N6 | Fingerlings |
| N7–N8 | Juveniles |

---

# **6.2 BROODSTOCK & EGG PRODUCTION**

## **Target**

- **3,500 eggs per month**

## **Breeding Ratio**

- 1 male : 4 females

## **Daily Tasks**

- Observe spawning behavior
- Identify holding females
- Check feeding response
- Remove mortalities

## **Weekly Tasks**

- Inspect fish condition
- Count holding females
- Collect eggs
- Record production

## **Monthly Tasks**

- Total egg count vs 3,500 target
- Remove poor breeders
- Replace if needed

---

# **6.3 NURSERY REARING**

## **Rules**

- Max density: **≤40 kg/m³**
- Up to 2 batches per tank if sizes match

## **Grading**

- Monthly (mandatory)
- Separate:
	- Fast growers
		- Medium
		- Slow

---

## **Daily Tasks**

- Check DO and flow
- Observe feeding
- Remove mortalities
- Check uniformity

## **Weekly Tasks**

- Inspect tanks and sludge
- Adjust feeding
- Check density

## **Monthly Tasks**

- Full grading
- Move fish to next stage
- Update biomass

---

# **7\. GROW-OUT SOP**

## **Stocking**

- Only graded fish enter grow-out

## **Density**

- Early: 30–40 kg/m³
- Final: up to 60 kg/m³

## **Daily Tasks**

- Monitor fish behavior
- Check feeding response
- Inspect aeration
- Remove mortalities

## **Weekly Tasks**

- Biomass estimation
- Adjust feed
- Inspect sludge

## **Monthly Tasks**

- Harvest planning
- Growth evaluation
- Tank rotation

---

# **8\. TANK ROTATION PLAN**

## **Monthly Cycle Rule**

Each month:

1. New eggs → hatchery
2. Fry → nursery early tanks
3. Nursery tanks shift forward
4. Month 4 fish → grow-out
5. Oldest grow-out batch → harvest

---

## **Grow-out Rotation (4 Tanks, 2 Cohorts Each)**

Each tank holds:

- One younger cohort
- One older cohort

This ensures:

- Continuous harvest
- Full system utilization

---

# **9\. FEED MANAGEMENT**

## **Manual Feeding**

| Stage | Frequency |
| --- | --- |
| Hatchery fry | 4–6 times/day |
| Nursery | 3–4 times/day |
| Grow-out | 2–3 times/day |

## **Rules**

- Feed based on appetite, not fixed only
- Avoid uneaten feed
- Track feed per tank

---

# **10\. MORTALITY & LOSS MANAGEMENT**

## **Tracking**

- Daily per tank
- Monthly survival rates

## **Action**

If losses increase:

- Check DO immediately
- Check flow
- Reduce density
- Inspect feeding

---

# **11\. RECORD KEEPING**

Each tank must track:

- Stocking date
- Fish count
- Average weight
- Biomass
- Feed input
- Mortality
- Movement history

---

# **12\. CRITICAL RISKS**

- Oxygen failure
- Overcrowding
- Poor grading
- Inconsistent egg production
- Overfeeding

---

# **13\. SYSTEM CONTROL PRIORITIES**

1. Oxygen stability
2. Water flow
3. Grading discipline
4. Feed management
5. Broodstock performance

---

# **NEXT STEP (OPTIONAL BUT RECOMMENDED)**

We can now refine this further by adding:

- Exact **month-by-month tank allocation table**
- Biomass per tank at each stage
- Feed chart aligned to your original table
- Emergency SOP (power failure, pump failure, oxygen crash)

---

This is now a **complete, functional SOP baseline** for your customer that can be expanded as real data comes in.

# **REFINEMENT 1: PRODUCTION PIPELINE STRUCTURE**

## **Cycle Length**

- Total: **10–11 months**
- Monthly staggered batches

## **Monthly Output Target**

- **600 kg/month**
- At ~700 g average → **~850–900 fish/month harvested**

## **Egg Input**

- **3,500 eggs/month**

---

# **REFINEMENT 2: COHORT MODEL (CORE LOGIC)**

Each month = **1 new cohort enters system**

Each cohort moves:

- Hatchery → Nursery (4 stages) → Grow-out → Harvest

So at steady state:

- You will have **10–11 active cohorts simultaneously**

---

# **REFINEMENT 3: NURSERY TANK ALLOCATION (LOCKED)**

You have **6 active rearing tanks (after broodstock)**

We assign:

| Tank | Stage | Cohort Age |
| --- | --- | --- |
| N3 | Early fry | Month 1 |
| N4 | Early fry (split batch) | Month 1 |
| N5 | Fingerling | Month 2 |
| N6 | Fingerling | Month 2 |
| N7 | Juvenile | Month 3 |
| N8 | Pre-growout | Month 4 |

### **Important**

- Two tanks per early stages allow **grading separation**
- By Month 4 fish must exit nursery

---

# **REFINEMENT 4: GROW-OUT STRUCTURE**

You have:

- 4 × 30,000 L tanks

We implement **dual-cohort per tank**

---

## **Grow-out Allocation Table**

| Tank | Cohort A | Cohort B |
| --- | --- | --- |
| GT1 | Month 5–7 | Month 8–10 |
| GT2 | Month 6–8 | Month 9–11 |
| GT3 | Month 7–9 | Month 10–11 |
| GT4 | Month 8–10 | Month 11 (harvest staging) |

---

## **Outcome**

- One cohort reaches harvest **every month**
- Tanks remain continuously loaded

---

# **REFINEMENT 5: MONTHLY MOVEMENT LOGIC (VERY IMPORTANT)**

At the **start of every month**, execute:

### Step 1 – Hatchery Output

- Transfer fry → N3/N4

### Step 2 – Nursery Shift

- N3 → N5
- N5 → N7
- N7 → Grow-out entry

### Step 3 – Grow-out Shift

- Oldest cohort advances within tank
- New cohort added where space allows

### Step 4 – Harvest

- Remove **oldest grow-out cohort (~10–11 months)**

---

# **REFINEMENT 6: BIOMASS TARGET PER STAGE**

## Nursery (2,500 L tanks)

| Stage | Target Biomass |
| --- | --- |
| Early fry | 5–15 kg |
| Fingerling | 15–50 kg |
| Juvenile | 50–100 kg |
| Pre-growout | 100–150 kg |

---

## Grow-out (30,000 L tanks)

| Stage | Biomass |
| --- | --- |
| Entry | 150–300 kg |
| Mid | 300–900 kg |
| Late | 900–1,800 kg |

---

# **REFINEMENT 7: FEED ALIGNMENT (SIMPLIFIED CONTROL)**

Instead of fixed feeding only, SOP will state:

## Feed Rule:

- Feed **% of biomass**
- Adjust weekly

| Stage | Feed % Body Weight |
| --- | --- |
| Fry | 10–15% |
| Fingerling | 5–8% |
| Juvenile | 3–5% |
| Grow-out | 1–3% |

---

# **REFINEMENT 8: GRADING CONTROL (CRITICAL SECTION)**

## Monthly Mandatory Grading

At every movement stage:

- Split into:
	- Fast growers → move forward
		- Medium → standard track
		- Slow → hold back (do NOT mix)

## Rule:

> Never allow size variance >30% within a tank

---

# **REFINEMENT 9: SURVIVAL MODEL (REALISTIC)**

| Stage | Survival |
| --- | --- |
| Egg → Fry | 75% |
| Fry → Fingerling | 70% |
| Fingerling → Grow-out | 80% |
| Grow-out → Harvest | 85% |

### Result:

- 3,500 eggs → ~1,200–1,500 harvest fish
- Supports **600 kg target safely**

---

# **REFINEMENT 10: CONTROL POINTS (SYSTEM STABILITY)**

## Must Monitor Closely:

1. **DO (oxygen)**
2. **Flow rate (your 60-min turnover)**
3. **Stocking density**
4. **Feed response**
5. **Grading accuracy**

---

# **REFINEMENT 11: FAILURE MODES (ADD TO SOP)**

## Oxygen Crash

- Immediate: stop feeding
- Increase aeration
- Reduce biomass if needed

## Overcrowding

- Emergency grading
- Split tanks

## Poor Growth

- Check feed rate
- Check density
- Check temperature

## Low Egg Production

- Adjust broodstock ratio
- Improve feeding
- Replace breeders

#### You said:

Option A And B and please i see you still have not added the egg production to the nursory i dont have more tanks we should use them all

  

#### ChatGPT said:

Correct. The nursery plan should explicitly include **egg production**, and **all 8 nursery tanks should be treated as active assets**. Two of them are not “lost”; they are the **broodstock and egg-production section** that feeds the whole pipeline. Tilapia broodstock have relatively low fecundity compared with many cultured fish, so broodstock space has to be built into the operating plan, not treated as an optional extra. FAO notes that egg number rises strongly with female size, from roughly **100 eggs/spawn at 100 g female size** to about **1,000–1,500 eggs/spawn at 600–1,000 g**, and also notes that sex ratios beyond **1:5 male:female** can overwork males. [FAOHome+2FAOHome+2](https://www.fao.org/fishery/docs/CDrom/aquaculture/I1129m/file/en/en_niletilapia.htm?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

So the corrected structure is:

- **N1–N2** = broodstock + spawning + egg collection
- **N3–N8** = fry, fingerling, juvenile, pre-grow-out, and buffer/held-back grading space

That uses **all 8 tanks**.

---

# Refined Option A + B

## 1\. Final nursery allocation using all 8 tanks

### **N1 – Broodstock Tank A**

Primary breeding tank.

### **N2 – Broodstock Tank B**

Second breeding tank and rotation/conditioning tank.

### **N3 – Early Fry Tank**

Newest hatchery output.

### **N4 – Fry / Split-Grade Tank**

Used for splitting fast vs slow fry, or as overflow from N3.

### **N5 – Fingerling Tank 1**

Main month-2 cohort tank.

### **N6 – Fingerling Tank 2 / Split-Grade**

Used for grading separation or second cohort of similar size.

### **N7 – Juvenile Tank**

Month-3 cohort.

### **N8 – Pre-Grow-Out / Buffer Tank**

Month-4 cohort, held-back fish, or pre-transfer conditioning before grow-out.

This is the cleanest way to integrate broodstock without adding tanks.

---

## 2\. The reason broodstock must stay in N1 and N2

To hit **3,500 eggs/month**, the breeding section must run continuously, not intermittently. Nile tilapia females spawn asynchronously and can spawn repeatedly when conditions are suitable; the practical effect is that egg collection should be scheduled every week, not treated as a once-a-month event. Published reviews report clutch sizes ranging widely, with a mean around **853 eggs/clutch**, and note that females commonly spawn at roughly **3–4 week intervals** under suitable conditions. [FAOHome+1](https://www.fao.org/fishery/ar/openasfa/4df5325b-c487-4ced-88f5-7edafc98ba16?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

So N1 and N2 are production tanks. They are not spare tanks.

---

## 3\. Broodstock sizing for a 3,500 egg/month target

This next part is an operational inference from the sources and your target.

If medium to large females are producing roughly **500–1,000+ eggs per spawn** depending on size and condition, then the theoretical minimum number of productive females needed per month is low. But theory is not the right basis for an SOP. In practice you need allowance for:

- females not spawning on schedule,
- unequal clutch size,
- recovery time,
- poor egg quality from some females,
- handling loss,
- broodfish replacement.

So for this SOP, I recommend starting with:

- **20 females**
- **5 males**
- ratio **1:4**

This is a conservative operational broodstock group for your target, and it fits your two 2,500 L broodstock tanks comfortably if biomass is controlled. The source support is the broodstock sex-ratio guidance and the relatively low fecundity of tilapia compared with other farmed fish. [FAOHome+2FAOHome+2](https://www.fao.org/fishery/docs/CDrom/T583/root/05.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

### Practical broodstock operating rule

- Keep **10 females + 2 or 3 males** in **N1**
- Keep **10 females + 2 or 3 males** in **N2**
- Replace poor spawners progressively, not all at once

That gives you redundancy. If one tank underperforms, the other still contributes eggs.

---

## 4\. Egg-production SOP inside the nursery

## **Broodstock tanks: N1 and N2**

### Daily

- Check temperature, aeration, flow
- Observe courtship, aggression, injury
- Observe feeding response
- Identify probable mouthbrooding females
- Remove mortalities
- Record abnormalities

### Three times per week

- Inspect females for mouthbrooding/holding
- Strip or collect eggs according to handling procedure
- Count eggs collected
- Transfer eggs to hatchery cups

### Weekly

- Log eggs collected from N1
- Log eggs collected from N2
- Compare weekly total to monthly run-rate target

### Monthly

- Total egg count target: **3,500 eggs**
- Remove weak or non-performing females
- Remove aggressive or poor-performing males
- Review hatch success from each broodstock tank separately

---

## 5\. Tank-by-tank rolling monthly schedule

This is the operational schedule you asked for.

Each month, one new cohort enters. I’ll call them **C1, C2, C3...**

At steady state, the nursery works as a **rolling conveyor**:

| Tank | Function | Current cohort position |
| --- | --- | --- |
| N1 | Broodstock A | Continuous |
| N2 | Broodstock B | Continuous |
| N3 | Early fry | newest cohort |
| N4 | Split fry / overflow | newest cohort slow grade or prior fry |
| N5 | Fingerling 1 | previous month cohort |
| N6 | Fingerling 2 / split grade | previous month cohort or slow grade |
| N7 | Juvenile | two-month-old cohort |
| N8 | Pre-grow-out / buffer | three-month-old cohort |

### Month-by-month movement rule

At the start of each month:

- Hatchery output of newest cohort goes to **N3**
- If fry are uneven, split to **N4**
- The prior cohort in **N3/N4** moves to **N5/N6**
- The prior cohort in **N5/N6** moves to **N7**
- The prior cohort in **N7** moves to **N8**
- The prior cohort in **N8** moves to **grow-out**
- Held-back slow growers remain in the split-grade/buffer tank instead of contaminating the next uniform tank

That is how egg production and nursery rearing coexist in the same 8-tank nursery.

---

## 6\. Example 6-month rolling map

### **Month 1**

- N1: broodstock
- N2: broodstock
- N3: C1 fry
- N4: empty / split-grade
- N5–N8: empty

### **Month 2**

- N1: broodstock
- N2: broodstock
- N3: C2 fry
- N4: C2 split fry if needed
- N5: C1 fingerlings
- N6: C1 split-grade if needed
- N7–N8: empty

### **Month 3**

- N1: broodstock
- N2: broodstock
- N3: C3 fry
- N4: C3 split fry
- N5: C2 fingerlings
- N6: C2 split-grade
- N7: C1 juveniles
- N8: empty / buffer

### **Month 4**

- N1: broodstock
- N2: broodstock
- N3: C4 fry
- N4: C4 split fry
- N5: C3 fingerlings
- N6: C3 split-grade
- N7: C2 juveniles
- N8: C1 pre-grow-out

### **Month 5**

- N1: broodstock
- N2: broodstock
- N3: C5 fry
- N4: C5 split fry
- N5: C4 fingerlings
- N6: C4 split-grade
- N7: C3 juveniles
- N8: C2 pre-grow-out
- Grow-out entry: C1

### **Month 6**

- N1: broodstock
- N2: broodstock
- N3: C6 fry
- N4: C6 split fry
- N5: C5 fingerlings
- N6: C5 split-grade
- N7: C4 juveniles
- N8: C3 pre-grow-out
- Grow-out entry: C2

After that, the pattern repeats.

---

## 7\. Grow-out schedule linked to nursery output

Because you have 4 grow-out tanks and a long cycle, each grow-out tank must hold staggered cohorts, but not mixed-size chaos. The rule is:

- one **older cohort zone**
- one **younger cohort zone**
- strict grading before transfer
- no severe size mismatch

A workable structure is:

| Grow-out tank | Primary use |
| --- | --- |
| GT1 | newest grow-out entrants + younger fish |
| GT2 | intermediate grow-out |
| GT3 | late grow-out |
| GT4 | harvest-stage fish |

### Monthly grow-out movement

- Fish leaving **N8** enter **GT1**
- As biomass builds, the oldest biomass advances toward **GT4**
- Harvest comes off **GT4** monthly
- Slow growers are not mixed back with harvest fish; they are retained and reclassified

This should be written into the SOP as a **movement principle**, not a rigid promise that every fish in a tank is identical. The essential control point is grading.

---

## 8\. Nursery biomass logic with broodstock included

For the SOP, I recommend these working biomass bands:

### N1–N2 broodstock

- operate conservatively
- keep broodstock below the nursery’s rearing-density ceiling
- prioritize spawning performance over pushing biomass

### N3–N4 early fry

- low biomass, high observation
- this is a management-sensitive stage

### N5–N6 fingerlings

- moderate biomass
- grade aggressively

### N7 juveniles

- rising biomass
- prepare for transfer

### N8 pre-grow-out

- highest nursery biomass
- final grading before grow-out

The reason to keep N1 and N2 conservative is that crowding can suppress reproductive behavior; dense tank culture can disrupt breeding behavior in tilapia. [Aquaculture](https://aquaculture.mgcafe.uky.edu/sites/aquaculture.ca.uky.edu/files/srac_282_tank_culture_of_tilapia.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

---

## 9\. Broodstock feed and spawning control

FAO guidance and related hatchery material support more frequent feeding in fry and more moderate feeding later, while broodstock output depends materially on condition and feeding quality. [FAOHome+1](https://www.fao.org/4/t8389e/T8389E11.htm?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

For the SOP:

### Broodstock

- feed **2 times/day**
- maintain body condition, not fatness
- remove eggs regularly to shorten the effective interval before the next spawn cycle where your handling protocol allows; hatchery literature notes that removing eggs from mouthbrooding females can increase spawning frequency. [ResearchGate](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/230337701_Improving_spawning_synchrony_in_the_Nile_tilapia_Oreochromis_niloticus_L?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

### Fry

- **4–6 feeds/day** is appropriate in intensive culture guidance. [FAOHome](https://www.fao.org/4/t8389e/T8389E11.htm?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

### Fingerlings / juveniles

- **3–4 feeds/day**

### Grow-out

- **2–3 feeds/day**

---

## 10\. What changes in the final SOP text

The final SOP should no longer say only “nursery rearing.” It should say:

### **Section 6: Nursery Operations**

#### 6.1 Broodstock and Egg Production

- N1–N2
- 20 females + 5 males start group
- 3,500 eggs/month target
- egg collection schedule
- broodstock replacement rules

#### 6.2 Nursery Rearing and Grading

- N3–N8
- rolling cohort movement
- monthly grading
- buffer handling for slow growers

That is the correct structure.

---

## 11\. My direct recommendation

For this client SOP, lock in the following:

- **Base harvest target:** 600 kg/month
- **Egg target:** 3,500 eggs/month
- **Nursery tanks all used**
- **N1–N2 broodstock**
- **N3–N8 rolling rearing**
- **Start broodstock at 20 females and 5 males**
- **Grade monthly, but split earlier if size spread becomes obvious**
- **Do not sacrifice broodstock space to temporary rearing pressure**, because that will destabilize the whole pipeline later

The next useful refinement is to turn this into a **single clean SOP document with a formal tank-allocation table and a monthly movement table**.

# **STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE (SOP)**

## Tilapia Production System – Eastern Cape Aquaponics

### **Document Status**

Version 2 – Operational + Production Model

---

# **1\. PRODUCTION OBJECTIVE**

## **Base Target**

- **600 kg Tilapia per month**

## **System Strategy**

- Continuous staggered production
- Monthly cohort system
- Internal egg production (no external fry dependency)

## **Scaling Path**

- Phase 2: 800 kg/month
- Phase 3: 1000 kg/month

---

# **2\. SYSTEM OVERVIEW**

## **Infrastructure**

### Hatchery

- 2 × 8L incubation cups
- 2 × 20L fry tanks

### Nursery (8 × 2,500 L tanks)

- Fully utilized:
	- Broodstock
		- Fry
		- Fingerling
		- Juvenile
		- Pre-grow-out

### Grow-out

- 4 × 30,000 L tanks

---

# **3\. WATER FLOW STANDARD**

## **System Rule**

> Minimum: **1 full water exchange per tank every 60 minutes**

## **Flow Rates**

| Tank | Volume | Flow |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Hatchery cup | 8 L | 0.13 L/min |
| Fry tank | 20 L | 0.33 L/min |
| Nursery | 2,500 L | 41.7 L/min |
| Grow-out | 30,000 L | 500 L/min |

## **Adjustment Rule**

Increase flow when:

- Biomass increases
- Feeding increases
- Oxygen demand rises

---

# **4\. FULL NURSERY STRUCTURE (ALL 8 TANKS USED)**

| Tank | Function |
| --- | --- |
| N1 | Broodstock A |
| N2 | Broodstock B |
| N3 | Early fry |
| N4 | Fry split / overflow |
| N5 | Fingerlings |
| N6 | Fingerlings split |
| N7 | Juveniles |
| N8 | Pre-grow-out / buffer |

---

# **5\. BROODSTOCK & EGG PRODUCTION**

## **Target**

- **3,500 eggs per month**

## **Stocking**

- 20 females
- 5 males
- Ratio: 1:4

## **Distribution**

- N1: 10 females + 2–3 males
- N2: 10 females + 2–3 males

---

## **Egg Collection Protocol**

### Frequency

- **3 times per week**

### Steps

- Identify mouthbrooding females
- Collect or strip eggs
- Count eggs
- Transfer immediately to hatchery

---

## **Monthly Requirement**

- Maintain **~875 eggs/week average**

---

# **6\. HATCHERY SOP**

## **Duration**

- Egg → hatch: 3–10 days
- Fry holding: up to 2–3 weeks

## **Critical Rule**

- Eggs must remain in constant gentle motion

---

# **7\. NURSERY PRODUCTION FLOW**

## **Stages**

| Stage | Tanks | Duration |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Fry | N3–N4 | Month 1 |
| Fingerling | N5–N6 | Month 2 |
| Juvenile | N7 | Month 3 |
| Pre-grow-out | N8 | Month 4 |

---

# **8\. MONTHLY COHORT MOVEMENT TABLE**

Each month a new cohort enters.

### **Movement Rule (Start of Each Month)**

| From | To |
| --- | --- |
| Hatchery | N3 |
| N3/N4 | N5/N6 |
| N5/N6 | N7 |
| N7 | N8 |
| N8 | Grow-out |
| Grow-out oldest | Harvest |

---

# **9\. EXAMPLE STEADY-STATE FLOW**

| Month | N3 | N5 | N7 | N8 | Grow-out |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| M1 | C1 | \- | \- | \- | \- |
| M2 | C2 | C1 | \- | \- | \- |
| M3 | C3 | C2 | C1 | \- | \- |
| M4 | C4 | C3 | C2 | C1 | \- |
| M5 | C5 | C4 | C3 | C2 | C1 |
| M6 | C6 | C5 | C4 | C3 | C2 |

Then continues indefinitely.

---

# **10\. GROW-OUT SYSTEM**

## **Structure**

- 4 tanks
- Dual-cohort system per tank

## **Flow**

- New fish enter GT1
- Progress toward GT4
- Harvest from GT4 monthly

---

# **11\. FEED MANAGEMENT**

| Stage | Feeding |
| --- | --- |
| Fry | 4–6x/day |
| Fingerling | 3–4x/day |
| Grow-out | 2–3x/day |

## **Rule**

- Feed based on appetite
- Avoid waste

---

# **12\. DAILY CHECKLIST**

- Check temperature
- Check aeration
- Check water flow
- Observe feeding
- Remove mortalities
- Inspect fish behavior

---

# **13\. WEEKLY CHECKLIST**

- Clean filters
- Inspect tanks
- Estimate biomass
- Adjust feed
- Check broodstock condition
- Record eggs collected

---

# **14\. MONTHLY CHECKLIST**

- Full grading
- Tank movement
- Biomass audit
- Egg production review
- Survival rate check
- Harvest planning

---

# **15\. GRADING RULES (CRITICAL)**

- Monthly minimum
- Separate:
	- Fast growers
		- Medium
		- Slow

## **Rule**

> Do not allow mixed-size fish to compete

---

# **16\. FAILURE MANAGEMENT**

## Oxygen drop

- Stop feeding
- Increase aeration

## Overcrowding

- Grade immediately

## Low egg production

- Replace breeders
- Adjust feeding

---

# **17\. SYSTEM CONTROL PRIORITIES**

1. Oxygen
2. Water flow
3. Grading
4. Feeding
5. Egg production

---

# **18\. FINAL OPERATIONAL PRINCIPLE**

> The system is only stable if broodstock, hatchery, nursery, and grow-out remain synchronized.  
> Egg production drives the entire system.

---

- **Aeration system sizing (blower + backup)**
- **Power failure protocol**
- **Water quality parameter ranges (NH3, NO2, pH)**

This document is now **fully operational and client-ready**, with both production logic and practical execution aligned to your system.

# **STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE (SOP)**

## Tilapia Production System – Eastern Cape Aquaponics

### **Document Status**

Version 3 – Operational + Explained + Field-Ready

---

# **ADDENDUM A: DEFINITIONS & ACRONYMS**

| Term | Meaning | Importance |
| --- | --- | --- |
| DO | Dissolved Oxygen | Critical for fish survival; low DO = immediate risk |
| FCR | Feed Conversion Ratio | Measures feed efficiency |
| Biomass | Total weight of fish in a tank | Drives feeding, oxygen demand, and flow |
| Cohort | Group of fish of same age | Used for tracking and movement |
| Grading | Sorting fish by size | Prevents competition and uneven growth |
| Broodstock | Breeding fish | Drives egg production and system continuity |
| Fry | Newly hatched fish | Most sensitive life stage |
| Fingerling | Small juvenile fish | Rapid growth stage |
| Juvenile | Intermediate growth stage | Preparing for grow-out |
| Grow-out | Final growth phase | Where biomass and revenue are generated |
| Stocking Density | kg of fish per m³ | Controls system stability |
| Turnover Rate | Time to replace full tank volume | Controls water quality |

---

# **1\. PRODUCTION OBJECTIVE**

## **Target**

- **600 kg Tilapia per month**

## **Why This Matters**

This target ensures:

- Stable system loading
- Manageable risk
- Consistent supply to customers

---

# **2\. SYSTEM OVERVIEW**

## **Purpose**

The system is designed as a **continuous production pipeline**, not a batch system.

Each section depends on the previous:

- Broodstock → Hatchery → Nursery → Grow-out

Failure in one stage affects all downstream production.

---

# **3\. WATER FLOW STANDARD**

## **Rule**

> Minimum: 1 full tank exchange every 60 minutes

## **Why This Matters**

Water flow:

- Removes waste (ammonia, solids)
- Replenishes oxygen
- Stabilizes temperature

Low flow = highest system risk after oxygen failure.

---

# **4\. NURSERY STRUCTURE (ALL TANKS USED)**

| Tank | Function |
| --- | --- |
| N1–N2 | Broodstock |
| N3–N4 | Fry |
| N5–N6 | Fingerlings |
| N7 | Juveniles |
| N8 | Pre-grow-out |

## **Why This Matters**

This layout ensures:

- Continuous egg production
- Controlled growth progression
- Space for grading separation

---

# **5\. BROODSTOCK & EGG PRODUCTION**

## **Target**

- 3,500 eggs/month

## **Why This Matters**

Egg production controls:

- Future harvest volume
- System continuity

---

## **BROODSTOCK DAILY CHECKLIST**

- Check water flow and aeration
- Observe feeding response
- Identify mouthbrooding females
- Check for injury or aggression
- Remove mortalities
- Record observations

## **Why**

Broodstock stress or poor feeding = reduced egg output.

---

## **BROODSTOCK WEEKLY CHECKLIST**

- Count holding females
- Collect eggs (3× per week minimum)
- Record egg numbers
- Inspect male:female ratio
- Check body condition

## **Why**

Regular egg collection:

- Prevents egg loss
- Maintains production rate

---

## **BROODSTOCK MONTHLY CHECKLIST**

- Total eggs vs 3,500 target
- Remove underperforming fish
- Replace breeders if needed
- Review hatch success

## **Why**

Broodstock quality determines system success long-term.

---

# **6\. HATCHERY SOP**

## **Key Rule**

Eggs must stay in **constant gentle motion**

## **Why**

- Prevents fungal growth
- Ensures oxygen exposure

---

## **HATCHERY DAILY CHECKLIST**

- Check temperature (27–30°C)
- Confirm egg tumbling
- Check flow rate
- Remove dead eggs
- Observe fry health

---

## **HATCHERY WEEKLY CHECKLIST**

- Clean filters
- Inspect air lines
- Record hatch success

---

# **7\. NURSERY OPERATIONS**

## **Purpose**

Convert fry → fingerling → juvenile → grow-out ready fish

---

## **NURSERY DAILY CHECKLIST**

- Check DO and flow
- Observe feeding
- Remove mortalities
- Check fish size uniformity
- Inspect tank cleanliness

## **Why**

Early detection prevents:

- Mass mortality
- Growth loss

---

## **NURSERY WEEKLY CHECKLIST**

- Check stocking density
- Inspect sludge build-up
- Adjust feed
- Inspect tank structure

---

## **NURSERY MONTHLY CHECKLIST**

- Full grading (mandatory)
- Move fish to next tank
- Update biomass
- Identify slow growers

## **Why**

Grading is the most important growth control tool.

---

# **8\. GROW-OUT OPERATIONS**

## **Purpose**

Maximize biomass and prepare fish for market

---

## **GROW-OUT DAILY CHECKLIST**

- Observe feeding response
- Check aeration
- Check water flow
- Remove mortalities
- Inspect fish health

---

## **GROW-OUT WEEKLY CHECKLIST**

- Estimate biomass
- Adjust feed rates
- Inspect sludge levels
- Check tank integrity

---

## **GROW-OUT MONTHLY CHECKLIST**

- Harvest planning
- Growth review
- Tank rotation
- Density check

---

# **9\. GRADING (CRITICAL CONTROL POINT)**

## **Rule**

- Grade at least once per month
- More often if size variation increases

## **Why**

- Prevents dominant fish suppressing smaller fish
- Improves feed efficiency
- Ensures uniform harvest

---

# **10\. FEED MANAGEMENT**

## **Rule**

Feed based on:

- Fish size
- Appetite
- Water conditions

---

## **Why**

Overfeeding causes:

- Water quality decline
- Oxygen demand increase
- Waste buildup

---

# **11\. TANK ROTATION SUMMARY**

## **Monthly Movement**

- Hatchery → N3
- N3 → N5
- N5 → N7
- N7 → N8
- N8 → Grow-out
- Grow-out → Harvest

---

## **Why**

Maintains:

- Continuous production
- Balanced tank loading

---

# **12\. RECORD KEEPING CHECKLIST**

Each tank must track:

- Fish count
- Biomass
- Feed input
- Mortality
- Movement history

## **Why**

Without records:

- No control over production
- No ability to improve

---

# **13\. FAILURE RESPONSE CHECKLIST**

## Oxygen Drop

- Stop feeding immediately
- Increase aeration

## Poor Growth

- Check density
- Check feeding
- Check water quality

## Low Egg Production

- Check broodstock condition
- Replace poor performers

---

# **14\. SYSTEM PRIORITIES**

1. Oxygen
2. Water flow
3. Stocking density
4. Grading
5. Feeding

---

# **FINAL NOTE FOR CLIENT**

This system is designed to be:

- **Stable first**
- **Scalable second**

Consistency is more important than maximum output.

---

# **ADDENDUM B: WATER QUALITY THRESHOLDS AND ALARM TRIGGERS**

## **Purpose**

Water quality must be managed as a **control system**, not just recorded as data. The purpose of thresholds and alarms is to identify problems early enough to prevent feed loss, growth suppression, spawning failure, disease pressure, and mortality. For Nile tilapia, the most operationally important parameters are **temperature, dissolved oxygen (DO), pH, ammonia, nitrite, and water flow**. FAO guidance places optimal temperature around **27–30°C**, DO above **5 mg/L**, pH around **6–9** or more tightly **6.5–8.5**, ammonia below **0.1 mg/L**, and nitrite below **0.2 mg/L**. [FAOHome+2FAOHome+2](https://www.fao.org/fi/static-media/MeetingDocuments/TiLV/s3.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

---

## **1\. WATER QUALITY CONTROL TABLE**

| Parameter | Target Range | Warning Level | Critical Alarm Level | Required Response |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Temperature | 27–30°C | <25°C or >31°C | <22°C or >34°C | Check heaters, inflow, weather effect, feeding rate |
| Dissolved Oxygen (DO) | 5–8 mg/L | 4–5 mg/L | <4 mg/L | Increase aeration immediately; stop/reduce feeding |
| pH | 6.5–8.5 | 6.0–6.4 or 8.6–9.0 | <6.0 or >9.0 | Check alkalinity, biofilter balance, source water |
| Ammonia (NH₃ / TAN trigger basis) | <0.1 mg/L unionized NH₃ target | rising trend or any reading near limit | ≥0.1 mg/L NH₃ | Reduce feeding, increase exchange, inspect filtration |
| Nitrite (NO₂⁻) | <0.2 mg/L | 0.1–0.2 mg/L rising | \>0.2 mg/L | Increase exchange, reduce feed, check biofilter |
| Nitrate (NO₃⁻) | keep low; preferably <27 mg/L | persistent rise | sustained high level with poor fish performance | Increase water exchange / plant uptake / solids control |
| Water Flow | 1 full exchange / 60 min minimum | 10% below setpoint | \>15% below setpoint or stopped flow | Immediate corrective action |
| Mortality | 0 or isolated losses | repeated daily mortality | sudden spike or cluster in one tank | Investigate tank immediately |
| Feeding Response | aggressive, complete feed uptake | slow feed response | fish refusing feed | Check DO, ammonia, temperature, disease signs |

These thresholds are conservative operational limits built from FAO tilapia guidance and standard tank-culture practice. Tilapia can survive outside some of these values, but production performance usually deteriorates well before lethal limits are reached. [FAOHome+3FAOHome+3Aquaculture+3](https://www.fao.org/fi/static-media/MeetingDocuments/TiLV/s3.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

---

## **2\. TEMPERATURE**

### **Target**

- **27–30°C**

### **Why it matters**

This is the preferred production band for Nile tilapia growth and general performance. FAO material also notes a broader survivable range, but growth and system predictability are best in the warm-water band around **27–30°C**. [FAOHome+2FAOHome+2](https://www.fao.org/fishery/docs/CDrom/aquaculture/I1129m/file/en/en_niletilapia.htm?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

### **Alarm triggers**

- **Warning:** below **25°C** or above **31°C**
- **Critical:** below **22°C** or above **34°C**

### **Required response**

- Check heaters and thermostats
- Check incoming water temperature
- Reduce feeding if fish are off feed
- Intensify observation of broodstock, fry, and hatchery first, because those sections are most sensitive to instability

---

## **3\. DISSOLVED OXYGEN (DO)**

### **Target**

- **5–8 mg/L**
- Never allow routine operation below **5 mg/L**

### **Why it matters**

DO is the most immediate life-support parameter. Tilapia are hardy and can tolerate low oxygen better than many species, but poor DO suppresses appetite and growth, and increases stress. FAO guidance for good tilapia production uses **\>5 mg/L** as the practical target, while training material also notes an optimal band around **6–8 mg/L**. [FAOHome+3FAOHome+3FAOHome+3](https://www.fao.org/fi/static-media/MeetingDocuments/TiLV/s3.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

### **Alarm triggers**

- **Warning:** **4–5 mg/L**
- **Critical:** **<4 mg/L**
- **Emergency:** fish piping, gasping, crowding near inlets, or feeding refusal even before the meter reaches the alarm point

### **Required response**

- Stop feeding immediately if DO is critical
- Increase aeration immediately
- Check blower, airline, diffuser, and pump performance
- Increase water exchange where possible
- Check if solids, feed waste, or overcrowding are driving oxygen demand

---

## **4\. pH**

### **Target**

- **6.5–8.5**

### **Why it matters**

Tilapia tolerate a fairly broad pH range, but production, nitrification, and general stability are better in the neutral-to-mildly alkaline band. FAO training material gives **6.5–8.5** as optimal, while other FAO material lists a broader acceptable range of **6–9**. [FAOHome+2FAOHome+2](https://www.fao.org/fi/static-media/MeetingDocuments/TiLV/s3.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

### **Alarm triggers**

- **Warning:** **6.0–6.4** or **8.6–9.0**
- **Critical:** **<6.0** or **\>9.0**

### **Required response**

- Check alkalinity and source water
- Check biofilter condition and recent feeding load
- Confirm pH at the same time of day, because pH can shift through the day
- Avoid abrupt chemical correction

---

## **5\. AMMONIA**

### **Target**

- **Unionized ammonia (NH₃): <0.1 mg/L**

### **Why it matters**

Ammonia comes from fish waste and decomposing feed. Even before a severe toxicity event, elevated ammonia depresses feeding and growth. FAO tilapia guidance commonly uses **<0.1 mg/L** as the operational upper target for ammonia. [Aquaculture+3FAOHome+3FAOHome+3](https://www.fao.org/fi/static-media/MeetingDocuments/TiLV/s3.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

### **Alarm triggers**

- **Warning:** any upward trend over consecutive readings, especially if feeding is also increasing
- **Critical:** **≥0.1 mg/L NH₃**, or fish showing stress with elevated total ammonia and high pH/temperature

### **Required response**

- Reduce or stop feeding temporarily
- Increase water exchange
- Check solids removal and biofilter function
- Check whether a recent grading, mortality event, or overfeeding caused a spike

### **Important SOP note**

If your meter or test kit reads **TAN (Total Ammonia Nitrogen)** rather than unionized NH₃, the actual toxic fraction depends on **pH and temperature**. In practice, any rising TAN trend should be treated as an early warning even before the calculated NH₃ fraction reaches the critical limit. This is an operational inference consistent with aquaculture water-quality practice and the fact that ammonia toxicity increases as pH and temperature rise. [Aquaculture+1](https://aquaculture.mgcafe.uky.edu/sites/aquaculture.ca.uky.edu/files/srac_282_tank_culture_of_tilapia.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

---

## **6\. NITRITE (NO₂⁻)**

### **Target**

- **<0.2 mg/L**
- Preferably keep as low as possible

### **Why it matters**

Nitrite is produced from ammonia during nitrification. It interferes with oxygen transport in fish and becomes especially problematic when biofiltration is unstable. FAO training material uses **<0.2 mg/L** as the practical target. [FAOHome+1](https://www.fao.org/fi/static-media/MeetingDocuments/TiLV/s3.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

### **Alarm triggers**

- **Warning:** **0.1–0.2 mg/L** and rising
- **Critical:** **\>0.2 mg/L**

### **Required response**

- Increase exchange water
- Reduce feed load
- Check biofilter status
- Review recent cleaning or disinfection actions that may have damaged nitrifying bacteria

---

## **7\. NITRATE (NO₃⁻)**

### **Target**

- Preferably **<27 mg/L**

### **Why it matters**

Nitrate is much less acutely toxic than ammonia or nitrite, but persistent buildup indicates insufficient plant uptake, insufficient exchange, or excessive feed loading. FAO training material recommends keeping nitrate below **27 mg/L** in tilapia systems. [FAOHome+1](https://www.fao.org/fileadmin/user_upload/faoweb/FI/news/VirtualCourseTiLV2021/Day5_1c.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

### **Alarm triggers**

- **Warning:** steady upward trend over several readings
- **Critical:** sustained high nitrate together with poor fish performance, poor plant uptake, or system loading pressure

### **Required response**

- Increase water exchange
- Improve solids management
- Review feed loading
- Confirm plant-side nutrient removal if linked to aquaponics

---

## **8\. WATER FLOW**

### **Target**

- **Minimum: 1 complete tank exchange every 60 minutes**

### **Why it matters**

Flow is not only for turnover. It supports oxygen delivery, waste transport, and temperature stability. If flow drops, water quality problems usually follow. [Aquaculture](https://aquaculture.mgcafe.uky.edu/sites/aquaculture.ca.uky.edu/files/srac_282_tank_culture_of_tilapia.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

### **Alarm triggers**

- **Warning:** actual flow falls **10% below setpoint**
- **Critical:** actual flow falls **15% below setpoint**, becomes intermittent, or stops

### **Required response**

- Inspect pump, valves, filters, inlets, outlets, and pipe obstructions
- Switch to backup pump or bypass if available
- Reduce feeding until stable flow is restored

---

## **9\. FEEDING RESPONSE AS A PRACTICAL ALARM**

### **Target**

- Fish should respond rapidly and feed aggressively according to stage

### **Why it matters**

Poor feeding response is often the first visible sign of low DO, ammonia stress, nitrite stress, disease, or temperature deviation. SRAC tank-culture guidance specifically notes that fish may stop eating aggressively when exposed to poor water quality such as high ammonia, nitrite toxicity, low DO, or high carbon dioxide. [Aquaculture](https://aquaculture.mgcafe.uky.edu/sites/aquaculture.ca.uky.edu/files/srac_282_tank_culture_of_tilapia.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

### **Alarm triggers**

- **Warning:** slower feeding than normal in one tank
- **Critical:** feed refusal, repeated leftovers, fish hanging low, fish clustering near inflow, or unusual surface behavior

### **Required response**

- Check DO first
- Then check flow, temperature, ammonia, and nitrite
- Do not continue full feeding until the cause is identified

---

## **10\. MORTALITY TRIGGERS**

### **Target**

- Isolated mortality only

### **Alarm triggers**

- **Warning:** repeated daily losses in the same tank
- **Critical:** sudden spike, cluster of mortalities, or multiple weak fish

### **Required response**

- Isolate tank as an incident
- Test DO, temperature, pH, ammonia, nitrite immediately
- Review feed, flow, aeration, and recent handling events
- Record incident and corrective action

---

## **11\. MONITORING FREQUENCY**

### **Minimum routine**

- **DO, temperature, and flow:** check **daily**, with special attention early morning
- **pH:** check **daily**
- **Ammonia and nitrite:** check **at least weekly**, and immediately when fish behavior changes
- **Nitrate:** check **weekly or biweekly**
- **After grading, transfer, mortalities, power interruptions, or feed spikes:** test more often

FAO training material emphasizes routine measurement of DO, temperature, pH, ammonia, and nitrite as part of standard farm record-keeping. Early-morning DO is especially important because oxygen is often lowest just before sunrise. [FAOHome+2FAOHome+2](https://www.fao.org/fi/static-media/MeetingDocuments/TiLV/s3.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

---

## **12\. SIMPLE ALARM RESPONSE HIERARCHY**

When a tank alarms, follow this order:

1. **Check DO**
2. **Check water flow**
3. **Check fish behavior and mortality**
4. **Check temperature**
5. **Check ammonia and nitrite**
6. **Reduce or stop feeding until stable**
7. **Record cause and action taken**

This order is practical because oxygen and flow failures kill fastest, while ammonia and nitrite usually rise as a consequence of loading, poor filtration, or poor hydraulics. [Aquaculture+1](https://aquaculture.mgcafe.uky.edu/sites/aquaculture.ca.uky.edu/files/srac_282_tank_culture_of_tilapia.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

---

## **13\. SOP INSERT: SHORT CLIENT WORDING**

You can insert this directly into the document:

> **Water quality thresholds are operational control limits, not only reference values.**  
> When a parameter reaches warning level, corrective action must begin immediately.  
> When a parameter reaches critical level, feeding must be reduced or stopped and the tank treated as an active incident until stability is restored.

---

# **ADDENDUM C: INCIDENT RESPONSE MATRIX (ONE-PAGE)**

## **Purpose**

Provide **immediate, clear actions** during system failures to prevent fish loss.

---

## **INCIDENT RESPONSE TABLE**

| Incident | Immediate Action (0–5 min) | Short-Term Action (5–30 min) | Follow-Up Action |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Power Failure** | Stop feeding immediately | Start backup power (generator) | Check all systems (pumps, aeration, temp) |
| **Pump Failure / No Flow** | Stop feeding | Switch to backup pump / bypass | Inspect blockage, repair pump |
| **Low DO (<4 mg/L)** | Stop feeding | Increase aeration immediately | Reduce density if recurring |
| **Oxygen System Failure** | Stop feeding | Deploy backup aeration (air stones, blower) | Repair/replace system |
| **High Ammonia** | Stop feeding | Increase water exchange | Check filtration + reduce feeding load |
| **High Nitrite** | Reduce feeding | Increase water exchange | Inspect biofilter function |
| **Fish Not Eating** | Stop feeding | Check DO first | Then check temp, ammonia, nitrite |
| **High Mortality** | Isolate tank | Test full water profile | Investigate cause + record |
| **Temperature Drop** | Reduce feeding | Check heaters | Stabilize gradually |
| **Temperature Spike** | Stop feeding | Increase flow / cooling | Reduce stocking pressure |
| **Blocked Drain / Overflow Issue** | Reduce inflow | Clear blockage | Inspect system design |
| **Hatchery Failure (low hatch)** | Check eggs immediately | Adjust flow + temp | Review broodstock + handling |
| **Low Egg Production** | Check broodstock condition | Increase feeding quality | Replace weak breeders |

---

## **Critical Rule**

> **If unsure: STOP FEEDING FIRST, then stabilize water quality**

---

# **FINAL PRINTABLE SOP (VERSION 4)**

---

# **STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE (SOP)**

## Tilapia Production System – Eastern Cape Aquaponics

---

## **1\. PRODUCTION OBJECTIVE**

- **600 kg Tilapia per month**
- Continuous production system using staggered cohorts
- Internal egg production (no external dependency)

---

## **2\. DEFINITIONS & ACRONYMS**

DO = Dissolved Oxygen  
FCR = Feed Conversion Ratio  
Biomass = Total fish weight per tank  
Cohort = Same-age fish group  
Grading = Sorting by size  
Broodstock = Breeding fish

---

## **3\. SYSTEM OVERVIEW**

### Hatchery

- 2 × 8L cups
- 2 × 20L fry tanks

### Nursery (8 × 2,500L tanks)

- N1–N2: Broodstock
- N3–N4: Fry
- N5–N6: Fingerlings
- N7: Juveniles
- N8: Pre-grow-out

### Grow-out

- 4 × 30,000L tanks

---

## **4\. WATER FLOW STANDARD**

- **1 full tank exchange per 60 minutes minimum**

| Tank | Flow |
| --- | --- |
| 8 L | 0.13 L/min |
| 20 L | 0.33 L/min |
| 2,500 L | 41.7 L/min |
| 30,000 L | 500 L/min |

---

## **5\. WATER QUALITY LIMITS**

| Parameter | Target | Critical |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Temperature | 27–30°C | <22 or >34 |
| DO | \>5 mg/L | <4 mg/L |
| pH | 6.5–8.5 | <6 or >9 |
| Ammonia | <0.1 mg/L | ≥0.1 |
| Nitrite | <0.2 mg/L | \>0.2 |

---

## **6\. BROODSTOCK & EGG PRODUCTION**

- 20 females + 5 males
- 3,500 eggs/month target

### Tasks

- Daily observation
- Egg collection 3× weekly
- Monthly performance review

---

## **7\. HATCHERY**

- Eggs tumbling continuously
- Duration: 2–3 weeks max

---

## **8\. NURSERY FLOW**

| Stage | Tanks |
| --- | --- |
| Fry | N3–N4 |
| Fingerling | N5–N6 |
| Juvenile | N7 |
| Pre-grow-out | N8 |

---

## **9\. MONTHLY MOVEMENT**

- Hatchery → N3
- N3 → N5
- N5 → N7
- N7 → N8
- N8 → Grow-out
- Grow-out → Harvest

---

## **10\. FEEDING**

| Stage | Frequency |
| --- | --- |
| Fry | 4–6x/day |
| Fingerling | 3–4x/day |
| Grow-out | 2–3x/day |

---

## **11\. DAILY CHECKLIST**

- Check temperature
- Check DO
- Check water flow
- Observe feeding
- Remove mortalities

---

## **12\. WEEKLY CHECKLIST**

- Clean filters
- Inspect tanks
- Estimate biomass
- Adjust feed

---

## **13\. MONTHLY CHECKLIST**

- Grade fish
- Move cohorts
- Review egg production
- Plan harvest

---

## **14\. GRADING RULE**

- Grade monthly minimum
- Separate by size
- Prevent competition

---

## **15\. SYSTEM PRIORITIES**

1. Oxygen
2. Water flow
3. Density
4. Grading
5. Feeding

---

## **16\. INCIDENT RESPONSE (SUMMARY)**

- Stop feeding first
- Stabilize oxygen
- Restore flow
- Check water quality
- Record incident

---

## **FINAL OPERATING PRINCIPLE**

> Stability first. Growth second. Scaling only after consistency.

---

This SOP is now:

- **Complete**
- **Client-ready**
- **Operationally usable**
- **Expandable**
